steps.title: Pin down Text 2's claim about King's Cross
steps.content: Text 2 says King's Cross provides access to the English countryside, which "fosters an idealism that values tradition, authentic personal connection, and the aesthetic" — what the novel calls "the infinite." King's Cross therefore functions as a gateway from London's materialism toward intangible, non-material value.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Because it is situated at the beginning of Margaret's journey from the city to the country, King's Cross emblematizes the intrusion of the forces of materialism and modernity into the rural spaces that the novel associates with idealism and tradition.’: This reverses Text 2's logic — King's Cross leads away from materialism, not into it. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘The austerity conveyed by King's Cross's appearance mirrors Margaret's disillusionment with the prospect of having authentic connections with other people in a world that chiefly values more conventional forms of prosperity.’: Text 2 says nothing about disillusionment; Margaret's feeling is wonder at "infinity," not discouragement. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘As a point of connection between London and the countryside, King's Cross suggests to Margaret the possibility of acquiring the intangible abundance promised by the kinds of authentic engagements that the novel's rural spaces seem to offer.’: Directly reflects Text 2's thesis that King's Cross is the gateway to "the infinite" through authentic, non-material connection. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘King's Cross has a relatively unassuming appearance whose sharp contrast with the more aesthetically pleasing appearance of St. Pancras suggests to Margaret the ascendancy of the pragmatic capitalist outlook among London's inhabitants.’: Text 2 does not frame King's Cross as a symbol of capitalist victory — it frames it as an escape from capitalism. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
